title: Research & Extension
text:
Scholarship & Outreach
My research and extension work address the same questions from different angles — one for academic audiences and one for the producers, lenders, and policymakers who need actionable economic guidance. Below you’ll find peer-reviewed publications alongside extension fact sheets, outlook reports, and decision tools.

title: Research & Extension
text:

title: Research & Extension
text:
Decision Tools


🐄 LRP Premium & Coverage Calculator


Estimate Livestock Risk Protection insurance premiums, coverage levels, and expected indemnity payments for feeder and fed cattle. Updated weekly with current CME prices.

Download (Excel)



📋 Cow-Calf Enterprise Budget


Customizable enterprise budget for cow-calf operations. Enter your own costs and production parameters to calculate breakeven prices and net returns per cow.

Download (Excel)



📈 Stocker Breakeven Calculator


Calculate purchase price limits, placement costs, and projected returns for stocker and backgrounding operations under alternative weight gain and price scenarios.

Download (Excel)



💧 Drought Herd Reduction Planner


A structured decision framework to evaluate the economics of early versus delayed herd liquidation under drought conditions, accounting for forage costs, cow values, and restocking outlook.

Download (Excel)

          on beef cattle markets, rangeland management, and risk management tools for cow-calf and
          stocker operators. My extension program delivers practical economic analysis directly to
          producers, lenders, and industry stakeholders across the region.

title: Dr. Andrew Anderson
text:
About
Background



I am an extension economist and associate professor in the Department of [Agricultural & Applied Economics] at [University Name]. My work centers on the economics of livestock systems across the Intermountain West and Great Plains — from cow-calf enterprise budgeting and stocker market analysis to federal grazing policy and drought impacts on ranch viability.
Before joining [University Name], I completed my Ph.D. at [University], where my dissertation examined [topic]. I was raised on a [cattle/ranch] operation in [State], and that practical background shapes how I approach both my research and my extension work.


Research Focus

Cattle market price dynamics and basis
Livestock Risk Protection (LRP) insurance effectiveness
Rangeland lease markets and carrying capacity
Drought economics and herd liquidation decisions
Federal lands grazing economics



Extension Work

Cow-calf and stocker enterprise budgets
Cattle market outlook publications
Ranch financial management workshops
LRP premium calculators and decision tools

title: Working Capital: The Number Your Lender Is Really Watching
text:
Replace with your own analysis.
After several years of strong cattle markets, some operations built up comfortable working capital cushions. But sustained input cost inflation and, in many areas, consecutive drought years have eroded those buffers. Lenders are watching this number closely heading into the 2026 operating loan season.

title: Working Capital: The Number Your Lender Is Really Watching
text:
What Is Working Capital?
Working capital is current assets minus current liabilities. On a ranch balance sheet, current assets typically include:

Cash on hand and operating account balances
Accounts receivable (cattle sold but not yet paid)
Prepaid expenses (feed, fuel, supplies)
Feeder cattle and stocker inventory
Growing crops or hay inventory (if applicable)

Current liabilities include the operating loan balance, accounts payable, and the current portion of any term debt coming due within 12 months.


Embed your R analysis or visualization here.

title: Working Capital: The Number Your Lender Is Really Watching
text:
The Working Capital-to-Gross Revenue Ratio
Raw working capital doesn’t mean much without context. A $200,000 cushion is comfortable for a 50-head cow operation and thin for a 500-head feedlot. The ratio to gross revenue scales for size:

Above 30% — strong position
10–30% — adequate, monitor trends
Below 10% — early warning territory; lenders will ask questions

title: Q4 Ag Credit Survey: What It Signals for Western Cattle Operations
text:
Replace with your own analysis.
The Kansas City and San Francisco Fed districts — the two most relevant to western cattle operations — both published their Q4 agricultural credit surveys last week. The headline numbers are mixed, with some improvement in loan repayment rates but continued softness in operating loan demand and a modest cooling of farmland values in drought-affected counties.

title: Teaching & CV
text: Teaching & Background

title: Teaching & CV
text:
Teaching Philosophy
My courses focus on applied economic reasoning for students pursuing careers in livestock production, ranch management, agribusiness, and agricultural lending. I use real market data, actual enterprise budgets, and current policy debates rather than textbook abstractions. Graduate courses use R for data analysis and applied econometrics.
